skill.name: flux-kontext
skill.description: Guides agents through targeted image edits with Flux 1 Kontext Pro on RunComfy. The skill documents model selection, prompt patterns, CLI invocation, input fields, output handling, and security notes for sending a single image and edit prompt to the RunComfy Model API.
skill.summary: Edit a single image with Flux 1 Kontext Pro through the RunComfy CLI.
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged many Markdown backticks, URLs, placeholder paths, and unrelated words as high-risk patterns, but I found no evidence of malicious intent or prompt injection in SKILL.md. The confirmed risk is operational: the skill intentionally uses the local RunComfy CLI, sends prompts and image URLs to a third-party model API, stores or reads a RunComfy token, and downloads generated files.

medium_findings.title: Intentional External CLI Invocation
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to install and run the RunComfy CLI. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it executes an external local command and depends on the behavior of the installed CLI.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command usage is explicitly documented and central to the skill. I found no evidence that prompt text is shell-expanded, but external command execution remains a real operational risk.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Image Processing and Token Use
medium_findings.description: The skill sends prompts and image URLs to RunComfy endpoints and documents token storage or RUNCOMFY_TOKEN use. This is expected behavior, but users should understand the data leaves the local environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly states that the CLI posts to the RunComfy model API and downloads generated results. Token handling is also documented, making the privacy and credential boundary clear.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Matches Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The many Ruby or shell backtick findings are Markdown inline code or fenced command examples, not Ruby backtick execution. They describe CLI usage rather than executable bundled scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: SKILL.md is documentation, and the flagged syntax appears inside Markdown code formatting. There is no Ruby source file or dynamic shell interpolation in the skill package.

low_findings.title: URL and Path Traversal Matches Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to RunComfy pages, GitHub, documentation, and the documented model API. The path traversal alerts correspond to placeholder HTTPS image URLs using ellipses, not filesystem traversal.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious path strings are examples such as HTTPS placeholders, not local file paths. The network endpoints match the stated RunComfy service purpose.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Alerts Lack Evidence
low_findings.description: No weak cryptographic operation or system reconnaissance behavior is present at the flagged lines. The referenced lines contain description text, model-selection guidance, use-case labels, or an exit-code heading.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: I checked the cited lines and found no cryptographic algorithm calls or reconnaissance commands. These appear to be pattern-matching errors against ordinary prose.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Required for Core Functionality
dangerous_patterns.description: The core workflow runs runcomfy commands locally. This pattern is acceptable for a RunComfy integration but should remain visible to users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and executable when copied into a shell. The documented JSON input lowers injection concern, but the command still runs a local CLI.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound Model API Calls and File Downloads
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow posts requests to RunComfy, polls for results, and downloads generated files into a user-selected output directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The skill states the outbound endpoints and output download behavior directly. The scope appears limited to RunComfy endpoints, but third-party processing is confirmed.

content.user_title: Edit Images with Flux Kontext Pro
content.value_statement: Precise single-image edits often fail when prompts are too broad or the wrong model is selected. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code clear RunComfy guidance for Flux Kontext Pro edits.
actual_capabilities: Selects Flux Kontext Pro for precise single-image local edits.
Documents the RunComfy CLI command for the model endpoint.
Explains required prompt and image inputs plus optional seed and aspect ratio.
Provides prompt patterns for preservation-led edits and small iterative changes.
Routes unsuitable requests to sibling models such as Nano Banana Edit or Flux 2 Klein.
Documents token handling, outbound endpoints, and generated file download behavior.
limitations: It supports one source image per edit request.
It requires the RunComfy CLI and a valid RunComfy account or token.
It sends image URLs and prompts to RunComfy for remote model processing.
It is less suitable for complex multi-step edits in one prompt.

use_cases.title: Product Label Adjustment
use_cases.description: Change a label, color, or small product detail while preserving lighting and composition.

use_cases.title: Portrait Accessory Edit
use_cases.description: Add or change one visible accessory while keeping identity, pose, and framing stable.

use_cases.title: Model Selection for Image Workflows
use_cases.description: Decide when Flux Kontext Pro is the right model and when another image model is better.

prompt_templates.title: Simple Local Edit
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for one targeted visual change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Flux Kontext Pro to edit this image. Keep the subject and framing unchanged. Add one clear visual change: [describe the change].

prompt_templates.title: Preservation-Led Edit
prompt_templates.scenario: Protect identity, product shape, or brand details during an edit.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Flux Kontext Pro. Keep [identity, pose, product shape, lighting, or framing] unchanged. Change only [specific area] to [specific result].

prompt_templates.title: Seeded Variant Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Create comparable variants for review using the same source and edit intent.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run Flux Kontext Pro with a fixed seed. Create a small variant set for this edit: keep [protected details] unchanged and adjust [target detail].

prompt_templates.title: Advanced Routing Decision
prompt_templates.scenario: Evaluate a complex image request before choosing the model.
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess this image-edit request. Use Flux Kontext Pro only if it is a single-source precise edit. If not, recommend the better RunComfy model and explain why.

output_examples.input: A product bottle needs its label text changed while the bottle, table, and lighting stay the same.
output_examples.output: The agent chooses Flux Kontext Pro, writes a preservation-led prompt, recommends a fixed seed for comparison, and saves the generated image to the chosen output folder.

output_examples.input: A portrait should show the same person holding a new umbrella with no other visible changes.
output_examples.output: The agent creates a single focused edit instruction that preserves face, pose, clothing, framing, and changes only the requested object.

output_examples.input: A user wants to combine several reference images into one edited result.
output_examples.output: The agent explains that Flux Kontext Pro is not the best fit and routes the request to a multi-image workflow instead.

best_practices: Use one source image and one clear edit goal per request.
State protected details before the requested change.
Use fixed seeds when comparing small variants.
anti_patterns: Do not combine several unrelated changes in one prompt.
Do not use this skill for multi-reference image composition.
Do not send private image URLs or sensitive prompts without reviewing RunComfy data handling.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It guides agents to run Flux 1 Kontext Pro image edits through the RunComfy CLI.

faq.question: Does it generate images from scratch?
faq.answer: No. It is focused on editing one existing source image.

faq.question: What tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow sends requests to RunComfy model endpoints and downloads generated outputs.

faq.question: Where is authentication handled?
faq.answer: The skill documents RunComfy login and the RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable for CI or container use.

faq.question: When should I avoid this skill?
faq.answer: Avoid it for multi-image edits, private data without review, or broad prompts that request many changes at once.
